message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>Covid-19 infection case predictions (total cases) are made for August through December 2020 for 10 US States (NY, WA, GA, IL, MN, FL, OH, MI, CA, and NC). A two-parameter model based on social distance index (SDI) and disease transmission efficiency (G) parameters is used to characterize SARS-CoV-2 disease spread. Current lack of coherent and coordinated US policy causes the US to follow a linear infection growth path with a limit cycle behavior that modulates the US between accelerating and decaying infection growth on either side of a linear growth path boundary.</jats:p>
                <jats:p>Four prediction cases are presented:</jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  <jats:list list-type="order">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>No school re-openings; fall season temperature effect</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>No school re-openings; no fall season temperature effect</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>School re-openings; fall season temperature effect</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>School re-openings; no fall season temperature effect</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>Fall outdoor temperatures, in contrast to the 1918 pandemic, are predicted to be beneficial for dampening SARS-CoV-2 transmission in States as they pass through “swing season” temperature range of 70F to 50F. Physical re-opening of schools in September are predicted to accelerate infections.</jats:p>
                <jats:p>States with low current infectious case numbers (eg, NY) are predicted to be minimally impacted while States with high current infectious case numbers (eg, CA and FL) will be significantly impacted by school re-openings.</jats:p>
                <jats:p>Updated infection predictions will be posted monthly (Sept, Oct, Nov, Dec) with adjustments based on actual trends in SDI and G. Assessments related to outdoor temperature impact, school re-openings, and other public gathering re-openings will be discussed in updated reports.</jats:p>
title: Prediction of Covid-19 Infections Through December 2020 for 10 US States Using a Two Parameter Transmission Model Incorporating Outdoor Temperature and School Re-Opening Effects

reference.unstructured: T. Newell , “Linking Outdoor Temperature and SARS-CoV-2 Transmission in the US Using a Two Parameter Transmission Model”, MedRxiv (non-peer reviewed), doi: https://doi.org/10.1101/2020.07.20.20158238, July 2020
91-divoc.com, Prof Wade Fagen-Ulmschneider, Computer Science Dept, University of Illinois
Maryland Transportation Institute (2020). University of Maryland COVID-19 Impact Analysis Platform, https://data.covid.umd.edu, accessed on [date here], University of Maryland, College Park, USA
